text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 23 August 2010 

Dr Sarah Ahmed 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St. Bartholomew’s Hospital, London  

Dear Dr Sarah Ahmed, 

Emma Carter, DOB: 12-06-1996, Hospital No: A837462 NHS No. 2045891173  
Flat 3, 22 Brook Lane, London, SE5 9QH  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with good response to Lamotrigine; Levetiracetam previously ineffective; Phenytoin not tolerated due to dizziness and cognitive slowing.

Aetiology: 

Perinatal infarct affecting the right parietal region (radiologically stable on prior imaging).

Other Medical Problems: 

Vitamin D insufficiency on supplementation; mild migraine without aura; no known drug allergies.

Background and Recent Events: 

She wears a smartwatch with fall-detection enabled; the device logs have been useful in corroborating the timing of events when available. In Mar she experienced a prolonged focal seizure lasting 8 minutes, resolving without intervention. In May there were three further brief absences, and in Aug a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure was reported at school (not witnessed by family). The school nurse documented a post-ictal phase of approximately 15 minutes; the smartwatch registered a hard fall alert at the corresponding time. There have been no emergency department attendances, and there has been no reported injury.

Current Treatment: 

Lamotrigine 150 mg mane and 200 mg nocte, single brand only. Buccal midazolam rescue pack held at school and home but not administered during the above events.

Management Plan: 

- Continue Lamotrigine at current dosing and maintain single-brand supply to avoid variability.  
- Arrange serum Lamotrigine level and vitamin D recheck prior to next review.  
- School has been provided with updated seizure first-aid guidance; smartwatch fall alerts to be printed from the companion app to support event chronology.  
- No driving. Occupational advice provided regarding graded return to PE participation at school with supervision.

Summary: 

This patient has focal epilepsy attributed to a perinatal parietal infarct. Event chronology over the past six months is as above, with smartwatch data aiding correlation of timings. No medication changes are proposed today given overall tolerability and absence of clustering or status features. I will review in six months or sooner if further generalised events occur.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter lists events over the past six months: one prolonged focal seizure in March (1), three brief absences in May (3), and one generalised tonic-clonic seizure in August (1). Total seizures over the past six months = 1 + 3 + 1 = 5. Therefore, frequency is 5 per 6 months. No indication of a longer seizure-free period.
